title: Empowering women facing gender-based violence amid COVID-19 through media campaigns
data_sources: Server visit logs and YouTube channel analytics

data_scope.population: female Facebook users in Egypt, all governorates
data_scope.platform_reach: ~45% of social media arm users visited hosting site; mean visitor watched 2-3 videos
data_scope.mobility_context: Egypt national mobility ~20% below pre-pandemic baseline during intervention (Google Mobility Reports)

core_conclusion: A pre-registered multi-arm RCT in Egypt (n=5,618) finds that WhatsApp outperforms Facebook in delivering a GBV edutainment intervention during COVID-19 mobility restrictions, while social media and TV reach comparable outcomes. The campaign increased women's knowledge of support organizations and their hypothetical and reported resource use, but produced no credible evidence of attitude change on gender norms or the justifiability of violence, underscoring the stickiness of entrenched social norms under repeated media exposure.
why_it_matters: First randomized evidence directly comparing WhatsApp, Facebook, and TV for GBV edutainment in an LMIC; separates knowledge/behavior change from attitude change; demonstrates digital outreach can substitute for in-person programming during crises; provides open data and code for replication.
why_nature: Pre-registered RCT with rigorous causal identification on a high-stakes social behaviour; cross-platform factorial design contributes to the science of digital behaviour; null on attitudes alongside positive behavioral findings speaks to theory of norm stickiness; generalizability to similar LMIC contexts globally.
added_value_to_my_research: 2SFCA/R2SFCA spatial reachability model for campaign coverage: treating social media reach as supply and vulnerable women as demand maps campaign accessibility gaps across governorates
Google Mobility time-varying index as treatment moderator: extends my spatiotemporal mobility-flow methods to behavioral-intervention moderation analysis
Prebunking inoculation design linked to spatial covariates: SHAP/MGWR analysis of where norm-change campaigns are most tractable produces a GeoAI intervention-prioritization map
Event-study of lockdown stringency on digital-resource demand: structurally equivalent to my demand-surge modeling in 2SFCA for primary care and mental health under capacity disruptions

Explainable spatial ML for NGO campaign targeting: SHAP-interpretable model of governorate-level treatment heterogeneity generalizes my EV-charging efficiency-vs-equity optimization to public-health campaign allocation

figures.caption: Demographic comparison (age, education, relationship status, children, social media use) between Arab Barometer and experimental sample — establishes representativeness among Egyptian female internet users
Treatment effects on TV show consumption, social media video consumption, and knowledge of support resources — all three arms show significant positive effects (p < 0.001)
Treatment effects on attitudes toward gender/marital equality, sexual violence, and willingness to donate — null results across nearly all attitude indices
Treatment effects on violence experienced during COVID-19 and hypothetical/recent use of online resources or support organizations — 4-6% increase in reported contact across arms
Treatment effects on women's future outlook toward gender and marital equality — individual delivery arms (SMI, TV) show increased optimism; WhatsApp Group arm does not
Comparison of baseline attitudes between Arab Barometer and experimental sample — sample is slightly more progressive than national average on gender norms
